text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Vitamin B12 absorption from eggs","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Vitamin B12 absorption from eggs\". _Proceedings of the Society for Experimental Biology and Medicine_. **149** (4): 987–990.","entailment":"The cited article title states that vitamin B12 is absorbed from eggs, supporting the claim that eggs provide absorbable vitamin B12.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Eggs from laying hens fed diets supplemented with chia, fish oil, and flaxseed have a specific omega-3 fatty acid profile","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Omega-3 fatty acid profile of eggs from laying hens fed diets supplemented with chia, fish oil, and flaxseed\". _J Food Sci_. **80** (1): S180–7.","entailment":"The article title indicates that feeding laying hens chia, fish oil, or flaxseed alters the omega‑3 profile of their eggs, supporting the claim about the profile resulting from those supplements.","population":"laying hens","intervention":"diets supplemented with chia, fish oil, and flaxseed","comparator":"","outcome":"omega-3 fatty acid profile of eggs","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Eggs from hens housed in conventional cage facilities differ in fatty acid, cholesterol, and vitamin A and E composition compared with eggs from hens housed in range production facilities","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Comparison of fatty acid, cholesterol, and vitamin A and E composition in eggs from hens housed in conventional cage and range production facilities\". _Poultry Science_. **90** (7): 1600–1608.","entailment":"The article title explicitly compares the composition of eggs from two housing systems, indicating a difference in fatty acid, cholesterol, and vitamin A/E content between them.","population":"eggs from hens housed in conventional cage and range production facilities","intervention":"conventional cage housing","comparator":"range production facilities","outcome":"fatty acid, cholesterol, vitamin A and E composition","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Eggs of caged hens and pastured hens differ in vitamins A, E and fatty acid composition","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Vitamins A, E and fatty acid composition of the eggs of caged hens and pastured hens\". _Renewable Agriculture and Food Systems_. **25**: 45–54.","entailment":"The article title reports a comparison of vitamin A, vitamin E, and fatty acid composition between eggs from caged and pastured hens, supporting the claim of a difference.","population":"eggs of caged hens and pastured hens","intervention":"caged hens","comparator":"pastured hens","outcome":"vitamins A, E and fatty acid composition","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Retail shell eggs have measurable physical quality and composition","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Physical quality and composition of retail shell eggs\". _Poultry Science_. **89** (3): 582–587.","entailment":"The article title indicates that the study measured physical quality and composition of retail shell eggs, confirming that such characteristics can be quantified.","population":"retail shell eggs","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"physical quality and composition","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Cooked and raw egg protein have different digestibility in humans as measured by stable isotope techniques","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Digestibility of cooked and raw egg protein in humans as assessed by stable isotope techniques\". _The Journal of Nutrition_. **128** (10): 1716–1722.","entailment":"The article title states that the digestibility of cooked versus raw egg protein was evaluated in humans using stable isotope methods, indicating a comparison of digestibility between the two forms.","population":"humans","intervention":"cooked egg protein","comparator":"raw egg protein","outcome":"digestibility","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Dietary cholesterol from eggs increases the ratio of total cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol in humans","claimType":"empirical","quote":"\"Dietary cholesterol from eggs increases the ratio of total cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol in humans: a meta-analysis\". _Am. J. Clin. Nutr_. **73** (5): 885–91.","entailment":"The meta‑analysis title reports that consuming cholesterol from eggs raises the total‑to‑HDL cholesterol ratio in humans, supporting the claim of an increase in that ratio.","population":"humans","intervention":"dietary cholesterol from eggs","comparator":"","outcome":"ratio of total cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"}]}
